notes.anchor: on the seventh day before the Ides of April
notes.note: Ante diem septimum Idus Apriles = 7 April 43 BC. Cicero writes this letter on 11 April (iii Id. Apr.); this date-within-the-letter records when he first received Plancus's dispatch.
T. Munatius (Plotius Plancus), brother of L. Munatius Plancus, then in Rome acting as his brother's political agent and liaison with Cicero and the Senate.
M. Caecilius Cornutus, praetor urbanus in 43 BC; in the absence of both consuls at Mutina he exercised consular authority at Rome and convened the Senate, following the mos maiorum.

notes.anchor: a religious objection was urged against Cornutus, on the warning of the chicken-keepers
notes.note: The pullarii were the official keepers of the sacred chickens whose feeding-behavior was an auspicial sign; if the chickens would not eat, the auspices were unfavorable (obnuntiatio). Cornutus's college of augurs (Cicero was a member) upheld the objection, delaying the session by a day.
The College of Augurs (collegium augurum), one of Rome's four great priestly colleges, responsible for interpreting auspices; Cicero had been an augur since 53 BC.

notes.anchor: a great struggle for me, on behalf of your standing, with Servilius
notes.note: P. Servilius Vatia Isauricus the younger (consul 48 BC with Caesar), who opposed awarding Plancus honors at this stage; he lost the vote when the Senate crossed to the opposing lobby (in alia omnia discessit).

notes.anchor: with Jupiter himself for an enemy --- in whose temple the business was being conducted
notes.note: Senate meetings were regularly held in the Temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus on the Capitoline; Cicero wittily says Servilius came hostile even to Jupiter on whose turf he was fighting.

notes.anchor: The third day before the Ides of April
notes.note: Ante diem tertium Idus Apriles = 11 April 43 BC, three days before the battle of Forum Gallorum (14 April); Cicero was writing in the tense days just before the decisive fighting at Mutina.
